application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We develop a magneto-elastic (ME) coupling model for the interaction between the vortex lattice and crystal elasticity. The theory extends the Kogan-Clem's anisotropic Ginzburg-Landau (GL) model to include the elasticity effect. The anisotropies in superconductivity and elasticity are simultaneously considered in the GL theory frame. We compare the field and angular dependences of the magnetization to the relevant experiments. The contribution of the ME interaction to the magnetization is comparable to the vortex-lattice energy, in materials with relatively strong pressure dependence of the critical temperature. The theory can give the appropriate slope of the field dependence of magnetization near the upper critical field. The magnetization ratio along different vortex frame axes is independent with the ME interaction. The theoretical description of the magnetization ratio is applicable only if the applied field moderately close to the upper critical field.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: It is well known that, the interaction between the defect-induced strain and the flux vortices in superconductors gives rise to the flux-pinning behaviors [1,2]. Similarly, the elastic deformation caused by vortices, the so called magneto-elastic (ME) interaction, affects the energy of the vortex lattice. The vortex-induced strain is due to different specific volumes between the normal phase (vortex core) and the superconducting phase (superconducting matter around the vortex). The normal-state vortex cores acts as homogeneous strain sources, generating local deformations in Magneto-elastic coupling model of deformable anisotropic superconductors deformation [16]. Besides, based upon the strain-dependent GL model, some analytical solution methods [17,18] for the ME interaction in superconductors with specific shape are proposed.

In this paper, we develop a ME model accounting for the interaction of the vortex lattice and crystal elasticity. The theory extends the Kogan-Clem's anisotropic GL model [19] to consider the ME interaction. The superconducting anisotropy and elastic anisotropy are simultaneously included in the model, in contrast to the previous theories only considering one type of anisotropy. The theoretical results are compared with some magnetization experiments, and the effects of the ME interaction as well as the theory applicability are discussed. The paper is structured as: in section 2, we establish the ME model and give the solutions near the upper critical field; in section 3, we apply the theory to calculate the elastic energy and magnetization; the theoretical results are compared with the relevant experiments in section 4; at last, we give the conclusions of the paper.

div.head: Anisotropic Ginzburg-Landau equations with strain effect
div.p: The GL theory with a phenomenological mass tensor ij M describes reasonably the major behaviors of anisotropic superconductors near the critical temperature c T . A new feature in deformable superconductors is the vortex-induced strain in terms of the elastic response of the crystal in the presence of vortex lattices. The free energy is [10] 2 24 1 * *

div.p: Here, (2 ) i e c ´ÇŻ ´Çş ´âĹ ´Çş ╬á A , A is the vector potential of the local magnetic field H , ´üÖ is the order parameter, ijkl ´üČ are elastic coefficients and ij u is the strain tensor. The inverse mass tensor 1 ij M ´Çş has the principal values 1 ( 1, 2, 3) i Mi ´Çş ´ÇŻ

. ´üí and ´üó are the coefficients in the GL functional expansion. In terms of the well-established strain dependence of c T , the vortex-induced strain enters in the free energy via the material characteristic ´üí [10]:

div.p: where the GL parameter ( , , ) x y z is obtained by rotating ( , , ) X Y Z with an angle ´ü▒ around axis Y . The axis z gives the vortex direction. Introducing the oblique coordinate system 1 2 3 ( , , )
x x x , the anisotropic ME problem transforms into a quasi-isotropic one.

div.p: Eqs. (5a)-(5d) describes the ME behavior of deformable superconductors in the presence of vortex-induced strain.

The solutions for the GL equations near the upper critical field 2 c H have been well established for the isotropic case [20]. Based on this consideration, we introduce a new coordinate system 1 2 3 ( , , )
x x x (Fig. 1) where the inverse mass tensor ij ´üş has the unit matrix form, i.e.

div.head: Solutions at the upper critical field
div.formula: 2 c H At 2 c

x y z , we obtain with the help of Eq. (A16):
div.formula: xz z x xx J J ´üş ´üş ´ÇŻ . (11b
div.p: Using the angular dependence of ( )sin cos cos sin
div.formula: z x J J ´üş ´üş ´ü▒ ´ü▒ ´üş ´ü▒ ´üş ´ü▒ ´Çş ´ÇŻ ´Çź . (11c
div.p: This reveals an important distinct feature in anisotropic superconductors at the immediate vicinity of 2 c H : the axial currents do exist in an array of vortices, and these currents vanishes when the vortices direct along one of the principal crystal axes a and c . In isotropic materials ( 13 ´üş´üş ´ÇŻ ), there is no axial currents at any vortex directions.

div.p: The general form of the free energy in anisotropic superconductors with elasticity effect is [16,19] 2 22 1 22
div.formula: z ijkl ij kl F H H u u ´üĚ ´üČ ´ü× ´ÇŻ ´Çź ´Çş ´Çź . (21

div.formula: 2 2 2 1 2 4 ijkl A z ij kl F B u u ´üó´üĄ ´üĚ´üČ ´üź ´Çź ´ÇŻ ´Çş ´Çź ,(22a)

div.formula: 2 2 2 ( ) ( ) 1 2 () ijkl zA z ij kl Ae B F B u u ´üź ´üó ´üĄ ´üČ ´üó ´üó ´üĄ ´Çş´Çź ´ÇŻ ´Çş ´Çź ´Çş´Çź . (22b
div.p: Now let us go further with the last term in the free energy (22a), considering the effect of the vortex-induced strain with the help of elasticity equations (6c) and (6d). The Fourier form of the vortex induced strain ij u reads [16] 0
div.formula: [ ( ) ( )] 2 i ij ij i j j i q i u u q u q u e ´âŚ ´é╣ ´ÇŻ ´Çź ´Çź ´âą q ¤ü qq ,(23)

div.formula: 0 ijkl ij ij u ´üČ ´üí ´üĚ ´Çź´ÇŻ ,(24a) 1

we have the strain field in deformable superconductors:

div.head: Comparisons with the results in the classical theories
div.p: Let us validate Eq. (20) [10]. Thus, we can neglect ´üĄ in the free energy (27b).
A ´üó ´ÇŻ is a geometric constant for the triangular lattice [20].
In deformable superconductors, the free energy (27b) depends on the strains via
div.formula: 2 2 2 ij e ij u ´üó ´üź ´üí ´üĚ ´üĚ ´ÇŻ´Çş with ij
div.p: ´üí given in Eq. (3b). For the uniform deformation, using Eq. (25a) we have
div.formula: ´üÁ´üÁ ´üó ´üź ´üó ´üČ ´ÇŻ ,(28)
div.p: where
div.formula: ij ijmn c mn T p ´üÁ´üČ ´éÂ ´ÇŻ ´éÂ . (29a
div.p: Since ij kl ijkl ´üÁ ´üÁ ´üČ is an invariant independent with coordinates transformation, we can calculate ij ´üÁ and ijkl ´üČ [in the new frame 1 2 3 ( , , ) x x x ] in terms of the crystal frame ( , , ) abc . mn p is the uniform stress applying on the superconductor, such that the nonzero components are 11
div.formula: ´üÁ´üÁ ´éÂ´éÂ ´ÇŻ ´ÇŻ ´Çź ´éÂ´éÂ ,(29b)
div.p: where ij C are the elastic moduli in the crystal frame. Thus, we have
div.formula: ij kl ab ab c c ijkl C C C C ´üÁ´üÁ ´üÁ ´üÁ ´üÁ ´üÁ ´üČ ´âŽ´âÂ ´ÇŻ ´Çź ´Çź ´Çź ´âž´âĚ ´âĘ´âŞ . (30a
div.p: or in an estimation for the order of magnitude,
div.formula: 2 ij kl c ijkl T p ´üÁ´üÁ ´üČ ´üČ ´éÂ ´âŽ´âÂ ´âž´âĚ ´éÂ ´âĘ´âŞ . (30b
div.p: where ´üČ is of the order of the elastic modulus.
Combining Eq. ( 28) with Eq. ( 30) determines e ´üó . So far, the parameters A ´üó , ´üĄ and e ´üó in the free energy have been determined.

div.head: Elastic energy
div.p: The elastic energy can be evaluated with the help of Eqs. (20) (26) ( 28) and (30b):
div.formula: ijkl e el ij kl z Ae F u u B ´üó ´üČ´üź ´üź ´üó ´üó ´ÇŻ ´ÇŻ ´Çş ´âę´â╣ ´Çź ´Çş ´Çş ´âź´â╗ . (31
div.p: Here,

div.head: Magnetization
div.p: Following the approaches in [19], one needs the expressions of the field H to obtain the magnetization
div.formula: ´ÇĘ ´Çę 4´ü░ ´ÇŻ´Çş M B H
div.p: . In the vortex frame ( , , ) x y z , the field components z H and x H are calculated by [19] ´ÇĘ ´Çę
div.formula: xz Mx xx Ae Ae BB H ´üş ´üź ´üź ´üź ´ü▒´üş ´üó´üó ´üó ´üó ´üź ´Çş ´éÂ ´Çş ´ÇŻ ´Çş ´ÇŻ ´Çş ´éÂ ´Çş ´Çş , (32a
) 1 2 z Mz z z Ae B F HB B ´ü▒ ´üź ´üó´üó ´âŽ´âÂ ´Çş ´éÂ ´ÇŻ ´ÇŻ ´Çź ´âž´âĚ ´éÂ ´Çş ´âĘ´âŞ . (32b
div.p: Now the magnetization M in the vortex frame reads
div.formula: 2 4 xz c x xx Ae HB M ´üş ´ü░ ´üş ´üó´üó ´Çş ´Çş ´ÇŻ ´Çş ´Çş , (33a
) 2 4 c z Ae HB M ´ü░ ´üó´üó ´Çş ´Çş´ÇŻ ´Çş ,(33b)
div.p: in conventional units. The ratio ( 1)sin cos cos sin
div.formula: x xz z xx M M ´üş ´üž ´ü▒ ´ü▒ ´üş ´üž ´ü▒ ´ü▒ ´Çş ´ÇŻ ´Çş ´ÇŻ ´Çş ´Çź . (34
div.p: is independent with the vortex structure parameter A ´üó and elasticity parameter e ´üó . This extend the conclusion by Kogan and Clem [19] to the situation with the ME effect: the magnetization ratio is independent with the ME interaction.
The magnetization components in the crystal frame ( , , ) X Y Z is obtained by using the coordinate transformation relations in Eqs. (33a) and (33b):
div.formula: ´üş ´ü▒ ´ü░´ü▒ ´üş ´üş ´ü▒ ´üş ´ü▒ ´üó ´üó ´üó ´üó ´Çş´Çş ´Çş ´ÇŻ ´ÇŻ ´Çź ´Çş´Çş . (35b
div.p: The magnetization ratio in the crystal frame is
div.formula: 2 3 1 tan tan X Z M M ´üş ´ü▒ ´üž ´ü▒ ´üş ´Çş ´ÇŻ´ÇŻ . (36

div.head: Angular dependence of the upper critical field
div.p: Ghosh et al. [21] measure the anisotropic upper critical field in CaAlSi. The experimental results can be fitted with Eq. (8b), by choosing the appropriate anisotropic parameter ´üž (Fig. 2). The increases in the anisotropy parameter ´üž with temperature coincides with the measurements in the same experiment. The similar temperature dependence is found in LaFeAsO 1-x F x thin films [22], ranging from 3.2 at 2 K to 4.2 at 15 K. However, for MgB 2 , ´üž is decreasing with temperature [23]. The electronic band structure and anisotropic nature of the order parameter are responsible for these behaviors. H ´ü▒ (dots) in CaAlSi single crystal [21]. The insert shows the temperature dependence of the anisotropy parameter ´üž , which is taken as the fitting parameter in Eq. (8b).

div.head: Field dependence of mmagnetization
div.p: The theory considers the mediate vicinity of 2 c B , and high-´üź material, such that from Eq. (32)
div.formula: ´üž ´üź ´ü▒ ´üž ´ü▒ ´ü▒ ´üž ´ü▒ ´ÇŻ´ÇŻ ´Çź´Çź ,(37c)
div.p: for calculation convenience.
According to the magnetization experiment [24] in La 1.45 Nd 0.40 Sr 0.15 CuO 4 [24], we take 10.5 ´ü▒ ´ÇŻ in Eq. (33).
div.formula: K c T ´ÇŻ , 0 (6K) 0.11 T c H ´ÇŻ , 0 (8K) 0.05 T c H ´ÇŻ , 0 (0) 0.2 T c H ´ÇŻ , 2 (6K) 4.8 T c c H ´ÇŻ , 2(
div.p: Since the mechanical parameters not involved in this experiment, we take H is relatively poor [Fig. 3(b)], since the slope of () MH is not properly evaluated.

div.head: Angular dependence of magnetization ratio
div.p: To check the anisotropic magnetization ratio Eq. ( 34), we fit Eq. ( 34) to the experimental magnetization data in YBa 2 Cu 3 O 7-x at 70K and 5T [25]. The fit gives the anisotropic parameter 4.5
´üž ´ÇŻ

[Fig. 4(a)]. This is consistent with the reported values 3~10 in other literatures [26][27][28] ´üž ´ÇŻ in Eq. (8b). Compared with the good-fitting range, the poor-fitting range shows several times higher values of 2 c H . Similar with YBCO, in this case, the difference between the applied field H and 2 c H determines the fitting accuracy. We also take 15
´üž ´ÇŻ to fit the data; this value approaches 17

div.head: ´üž ´é╗
div.p: proposed by Tuominen et al. [25]. This basically resolves the deviations in the region 60 120
div.formula: ´ü▒ ´Ç╝´Ç╝ . However, 2 c
div.p: H is nearly two orders of magnitude higher than the applied field. ´üž ´ÇŻ . It must be noted, the seemingly good fit by 15 ´üž ´ÇŻ is actually out of scope of the theory, so it masks the physics of the anisotropic magnetization.
We implement the fit for the Tl-2223 samples, which is supposed to have remarkably high ´üž and H ´ÇŻ [32]. In this case, the agreement is fairly good for 90 10

div.head: ´ü▒ ´Çş´é│
div.p: , independently of the ´üž value used above ~15 [Fig. 4(c)]. With higher ´üž value, i.e. ´üž =30 and 50, the amplitude of the peak approaches the experimental data at the vicinity of 90 . However, the high ´üž values lead to the 2 c H two or three orders of magnitude higher than the applied field 1 H ´ÇŻ in this region. So, the theory is not applicable in this case, even for the small-´ü▒ regime. This conclusion can also be verified by comparing Eq. ( 36) with the experimental [31] magnetization ratio along the crystal axes. Equation (36) establishes the angular dependence of XZ MM : a larger ´ü▒ corresponds to a higher ratio value. In contrary, however, the experiment [31] for Tl-2223 shows an opposite dependence.

div.head: Concluding remarks
div.p: Based on the anisotropic GL theory, we model the ME interaction in a uniaxial superconductor. The present model includes the anisotropic superconductivity and elastic deformation, by introducing the anisotropic electron mass and the elastic energy in the GL free energy. The GL equations are now correlated to the ME interaction. The solutions are obtained by using the anisotropic Abrikosov identities under the considerations of the elasticity effect. As a validation, the formulas of the free energy is compared with the classical results.

The elasticity parameter e ´üó , vortex-lattice parameter A ´üó and anisotropy parameter ´üĄ determined for practice. We derive the practical forms of free energy and magnetization. The formula of the anisotropic magnetization ratio remains its classical representation, even taking into account the ME coupling effect.

We compare the theory with the experiments. By including the ME interaction, the model gives a satisfied description of the field dependence of the magnetization near the upper critical field. In Fe-based superconductors, the strong dependence c Tp ´éÂ´éÂ leads to a remarkable ME interaction, which is comparable to the vortex energy. The ME coupling effect is important for an appropriate description of the magnetization behaviors.

The effect of the ME interaction is absent in the magnetization ratio xz MM along different vortex frame axes. This enables the fitting of the relevant experiments to determine the anisotropic parameter ´üž substantially. However, it should be noted that, excessively high ´üž values lead to unrealistically large 2 c H , especially when the field directs approximately in the superconducting layer ( ab plane). One must bear in mind the applicability range of the theory: H . Extending the theory to a moderate field range is significant for practice. However, in this case, the precise vortex structure and spatial distribution of order parameter should be considered. This makes the problem intricate, not to mention the anisotropies of elasticity and superconductivity. The anisotropic ME interaction in the moderate magnetic field range will be the focus of our next research. 0 0 0 0

div.p: The upper indices dictate the contravariant quantities. Supposing an array of vortices tilted from the crystal axes, we introduce a new coordinates ( , , ) x y z of vortices. ( , , ) x y z is rotated with respect to the ( , , ) X Y Z through an angle ´ü▒ about the Y axis (Fig. 1). When ( , , ) X Y Z transforms into ( , , ) x y z , the tensor components transform as the coordinates transformation relations 1 xz ´ÇŻ for the convenience of tensor analysis):

div.p: Obviously, the new coordinate system is oblique: the new x and 3 x axes are along with the old 2 x and 3 x axes, while the new 1
x axis is inclined with the 1 x at an angle ´ÇĘ ´Çę 1 =arctan cd ´ü¬ ´Çş (Fig. 1). The coincidence of the new 3
x and the old z axis is important since in the vortex problem near 2 c H , any z -independent quantity ´üĘ is 3
x -independent:

x x x relates to ij ´üş through the transformation relations:

div.p: where the invariance relations Eq. (A4) and 1 2 3 1 ´üş ´üş ´üş ´ÇŻ (obviously from the definition of the dimensionless inverse mass) have been used.
The metric tensor ij g of the new frame is obtained using Eq. (A8),
div.formula: ´üş ´üş ´üş ´üş ´üş ´üş ´üş ´üş ´üş ´üş ´üş ´üş ´Çş ´Çş ´Çş ´Çş ´Çş ´Çş ´âę´â╣ ´Çź ´â¬´â║ ´âę ´â╣ ´âę ´â╣ ´ÇŻ ´âŚ ´ÇŻ ´â¬´â║ ´âź ´â╗ ´âź ´â╗ ´â¬´â║ ´âź´â╗ gg . (A13)

tensor ijk e preservers its 0 , 1 ´é▒ form when transformed from ( , , )

x y z through the coordinate transformation relations: (1 )

figure.figDesc: as the units of the order parameter and magnetic field, respectively. As usual, one takes the average London penetration depth as the unit length. The units of the energy density f and elastic moduli ´üČ

figure.head: Fig. 1 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 1. (a) The crystal coordinate system ( , , ) X Y Z coincides with the principal axes of the crystal a , b and c in a uniaxial layered superconductor. (b) The vortex coordinate frame( , , ) x y z is obtained by rotating ( , , ) X Y Z with an

figure.head: Fig. 2 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 2. The upper critical field as a function of the angle 2 ´ü░´ü▒ ´Çş at temperatures 3.0, 4.2, 5.0 and 5.5 K. Eq. (8b) (lines) fits roughly well the measured

figure.head: ´üó
figure.figDesc: similar crystal La 1.45 Nd 0.40 Sr 0.14 CuO 4 as suggested by[5]. We obtain () MH shown in Fig.3(a). The calculation curve describes well the experimental data near 2 c H , but there are relatively large deviations away from 2 c H . This is very important since the theory is essentially established at the intermediate vicinity of . Since the superconductivity is sensitive to pressure in La 1.45 Nd 0.40 Sr 0.15 CuO 4 [5], i.e. considerable Without including the elasticity effect, i.e. setting 0 e ´üó ´ÇŻ in Eq. (33b), the agreement around 2 c

figure.head: Fig. 3 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 3. (a) The field dependence of the magnetization. The lines are obtained with Eq. (33b), to compare the experimental data in La 1.45 Nd 0.40 Sr 0.15 CuO 4 [24]. The arrows indicate the upper critical field 2 c H , where the magnetization increases to 0. The insert shows the magnification near 2 c H . (b) Comparison with the result of 0 e ´üó ´ÇŻ ,

figure.head: Fig. 4 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 4. (a) Angular dependence of the XZ MM ratio in YBCO crystal. The experimental data [25] (solid square) are measured at temperature 70K and magnetic field 5T. The line corresponds to Eq. (34) using 5 ´üž ´ÇŻ . The insert shows

al. [16]. On the other hand, if the superconducting material is anisotropic but without strain dependence, i.e.
. The last one coincides with Kogan-Clem's result [19].

div.head: Acknowledgments
div.p: This work was performed with supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11372120, 11421062, 11102171 and 11302119) and National Key Project of Magneto-Restriction Fusion Energy Development Program (2013GB110002). The Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (A0920502051619-95) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M602707) are also acknowledged.

org.orgName: National Key Project of Magneto-Restriction Fusion Energy Development Program

div.head: Appendix A: Transformation of coordinates
div.p: Let us consider a uniaxial layered superconductor (Fig. 1). In the crystal coordinate system ( , , ) X Y Z where Z axis is normal to the layers, and the components KL ´üş ( , 1, 2,3) KL´ÇŻ of the inverse dimensionless mass tensor

monogr.title: Fundamentals of the Theory of Metals
